title: Expressing Opinions
situation: You just watched a Bollywood movie with friends and everyone's sharing their take on it.

phrases.context: 'mujhe lagta/lagi' = I feel/felt, your go-to opinion structure in Hindi

phrases.hindi: yaar, first half boring tha na?
phrases.english: Dude, the first half was boring right?
phrases.context: 'tha' = was (masculine). 'na?' at end = seeking agreement, like 'right?'
phrases.pronunciation: YAAR, first half BO-ring THA na?

phrases.hindi: haan but end mein twist tha, woh mast tha
phrases.english: Yeah but there was a twist at the end, that was cool
phrases.context: 'mast' = cool/awesome (Mumbai slang, universal now). Mixing English words is totally natural.
phrases.pronunciation: HAAN but END mein TWIST tha, woh MAST tha

phrases.hindi: mujhe nahi lagta ki itni acchi thi
phrases.english: I don't think it was that good
phrases.context: Polite disagreement, 'mujhe nahi lagta' = I don't feel/think. Softer than directly saying 'it was bad'
phrases.pronunciation: muj-HE na-HI LAG-ta ki it-NI ac-CHI thi

phrases.hindi: acting toh kamaal thi lekin story weak thi
phrases.english: The acting was amazing but the story was weak
phrases.context: 'toh' emphasizes what comes before. 'lekin' = but, natural contrast structure
phrases.pronunciation: AC-ting toh ka-MAAL thi LE-kin STO-ry WEAK thi

phrases.hindi: tum batao, tumhe kaisa laga honestly?
phrases.english: You tell, how did you honestly find it?
phrases.context: 'tum batao' = you tell (friendly, peer-level). Asking someone for their take directly.
phrases.pronunciation: TUM ba-TAA-o, tum-HE KAI-sa LA-ga honestly?

phrases.hindi: haan sahi baat hai, woh scene toh next level tha
phrases.english: Yeah that's true, that scene was next level
phrases.context: 'sahi baat hai' = that's right/true, conceding a point. 'next level' used as-is in Hindi.
phrases.pronunciation: HAAN sa-HI baat hai, woh SCENE toh next LE-vel tha

phrases.hindi: mujhe toh neend aa rahi thi beech mein
phrases.english: I was falling asleep in the middle
phrases.context: 'neend aa rahi thi' = sleep was coming (how Hindi says 'getting sleepy'), brutally honest opinion
phrases.pronunciation: muj-HE toh NEEND aa ra-HI thi BEECH mein

phrases.hindi: accha woh chhodo, interval ke baad kya hua?
phrases.english: Forget that, what happened after the interval?
phrases.context: 'chhodo' = leave it/forget it. 'interval' = intermission (all Indian movies have one)
phrases.pronunciation: ac-CHA woh CHHO-do, IN-ter-val ke BAAD kya HU-a?

phrases.hindi: overall theek thi, ek baar dekh sakte ho
phrases.english: Overall it was fine, you can watch it once
phrases.context: Lukewarm recommendation, 'ek baar dekh sakte ho' = worth one watch (not great, not terrible)
phrases.pronunciation: OVER-all THEEK thi, EK baar dekh SAK-te ho

grammar_notes: Past tense uses 'tha/thi/the': masculine singular = tha, feminine singular = thi, plural = the. Matches the SUBJECT gender, not the speaker.
'Mujhe lagta hai' = I think/feel (present). 'Mujhe laga' = I felt (past, masc subject). 'Mujhe lagi' = I felt (past, fem subject).
'toh' adds emphasis, 'woh toh accha tha' = that WAS good (stressing the point). It's not translatable, it's a vibe.
'ki' connects clauses like 'that' in English: 'mujhe lagta hai KI yeh acchi hai' = I think THAT this is good.
Mixing English freely is not just okay, it's expected in urban Hindi. 'Boring', 'twist', 'acting', 'next level', all used as-is.
culture_notes: Indians are very expressive about movies, strong opinions are expected and part of the fun. Hold nothing back.
Using English words mixed in ('boring', 'twist', 'acting') is totally normal in Hindi conversation, it's called Hinglish.
Every Bollywood movie has an intermission ('interval'), comparing first half vs second half is a standard discussion format.
Rating movies as 'ek baar dekh lo' (watch once) vs 'must watch' vs 'mat dekho' (don't watch) is the standard scale.

skill_breakdown.explanation: Simple past: was/were. Masculine = tha, Feminine = thi, Plural/Respect = the. Matches the SUBJECT gender not the speaker. 'Film acchi thi' (film=feminine). 'Khana accha tha' (khana=masculine).

more_examples.english: who all were at the party?

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Lagta hai' = it seems/I think. Most natural way to express opinions without being too direct. Structure: mujhe + [adjective] + lagta/lagti + hai. Past: mujhe + [adjective] + laga/lagi. For other people: usko lagta hai = he/she thinks.

more_examples.hindi: mujhe lagta hai woh sahi bol raha hai

more_examples.english: how did you find it? (what did you feel?)

skill_breakdown.explanation: Agree: 'haan bilkul' (yes absolutely), 'sahi baat hai' (that's right), 'ekdum' (exactly). Disagree politely: 'mujhe nahi lagta' (I don't think so), 'haan lekin...' (yeah but...), 'ho sakta hai par...' (could be but...). Never say a flat 'nahi' to someone's opinion.

more_examples.english: no dude, it's not like that

more_examples.hindi: ho sakta hai, par mujhe alag laga
more_examples.english: could be, but I felt differently

practice_prompt: You are the user's close friend chatting on a Sunday afternoon about Jawan, which you both finally watched. You loved it, SRK's double role, action set-pieces, the social-issue twist, Anirudh's score. The user has mixed feelings (too long, second half drags, story over-the-top). Use 'tum' throughout, casual, slangy, peer. Code-mix English freely (first half, twist, vibe, climax). Open with 'yaar, woh Jawan dekh li na? mujhe toh bahut pasand aayi, tumhe kaisi lagi?' Share opinions using 'mujhe accha laga / nahi laga' and 'lagta hai'; frame past beats with 'tha/thi/the' (acting kamaal thi, climax mast tha, gaane acche the). Push back warmly, 'nahi yaar', 'haan but...', 'bilkul nahi', 'honestly', and concede where you agree ('haan woh toh sahi hai'). Ask the user back every couple of turns. If they slip (wrong gender on tha/thi, missing 'lagta hai' framing, English word-order in opinions), flag inline with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]] and keep going. Stay in Hindi unless asked. Keep it 6-8 turns and wrap with a jokey 'chalo yaar, apni apni opinion.'
